title: Indonesian Chicken Satay with Peanut Sauce

ingredients.name: boneless chicken thighs, cut into 2 cm cubes

subs.name: 500 g paneer cubes or tofu, marinated the same way for a vegetarian satay
subs.notes: reduce grill time to 2 minutes per side

ingredients.name: lemongrass stalks, white part only, finely grated

subs.name: 1 tsp lemon zest plus extra ginger
subs.notes: lemongrass is now available at many Indian supermarkets

ingredients.name: fresh ginger, grated (substitute for galangal)
visual_checks: galangal is traditional; ginger gives a close result
ingredients.notes: galangal is traditional; ginger gives a close result

subs.name: cashew paste (100 g blended cashews) for a peanut-free sauce

steps.text: Blend or finely mince garlic and grated lemongrass together. Combine with turmeric, coriander powder, soy sauce, half the jaggery and 1 tbsp oil into a marinade. Toss the chicken cubes in this mixture and marinate for at least 20 minutes at room temperature.

While the chicken marinates, make the peanut sauce. Blend the roasted peanuts until coarse crumbs, then add coconut milk, remaining jaggery, red chilli powder, lemon juice and a pinch of salt. Blend to a thick, pourable consistency — add water by the tablespoon if needed.
Pour the peanut sauce into a small saucepan and warm on low heat for 3–4 minutes, stirring, until it thickens and turns glossy. Taste and balance salt, chilli and sweetness. Keep warm.
Thread 4–5 chicken pieces onto each skewer (use soaked bamboo skewers or thin metal ones). Brush lightly with remaining oil.
Heat a tawa or grill pan on high until smoking. Grill the skewers for 4–5 minutes, turning every 90 seconds, until the chicken is charred at the edges and cooked through with no pink inside.
Rest the skewers for 2 minutes. Serve hot with the warm peanut sauce for dipping or spooned over the skewers, and sliced cucumber and red onion on the side.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: peanut → cashew paste (100 g blended cashews) for a peanut-free sauce (thinner sauce — add less water); chicken → 500 g paneer cubes or tofu, marinated the same way for a vegetarian satay (reduce grill time to 2 minutes per side); lemongrass → 1 tsp lemon zest plus extra ginger (lemongrass is now available at many Indian supermarkets); jaggery → 1 tbsp honey or brown sugar
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Use a cast-iron tawa or skillet on the highest flame — the char marks make a real difference. Bamboo skewers (soaked 30 minutes) or metal skewers work. Roasted peanuts (verkadalai, available everywhere) are the base of the peanut sauce. Jaggery (gur or vellam) replaces palm sugar perfectly. Lemongrass is now stocked in most metro Indian supermarkets; otherwise substitute with lemon zest.
